Adding up those charges from Feb 17 through Mar 8 gives me 22,590.06, which when combined with the outstanding balance equals exactly 113,943.62 — the amount of the Mar 13 payment. That payment brought the balance to zero, which confirms the transaction history is consistent.

Looking back at the full timeline from November, each payment has been precise: the Nov 14 and Dec 16 payments cleared the balance exactly, the Jan 6 payment actually overpaid by about 5,677 rupees, then Feb 11 created a new 14,814 balance that carried forward. That credit then got applied to the February 11 payment, reducing what was owed from ₹137,593.23 down to ₹131,916.68.

  { date:"2026-03-30", desc:"Trae",                    amount:970.80,     card:"4315", isPayment:false, isRefund:false },
];

// ── compute running balance ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
const sorted = [...RAW].sort((a, b) => a.date.localeCompare(b.date));

let runningBalance = 0;
const withBalance = sorted.map(tx => {
  runningBalance += tx.amount;
  return { ...tx, balance: runningBalance };
});

// ── aggregate by month ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
type MonthBucket = { month: string; charges: number; payments: number; refunds: number; netPayments: number };
const monthMap: Record<string, MonthBucket> = {};
for (const tx of sorted) {
  const m = tx.date.slice(0, 7);
  if (!monthMap[m]) monthMap[m] = { month: m, charges: 0, payments: 0, refunds: 0, netPayments: 0 };
  if (tx.isPayment) monthMap[m].payments += Math.abs(tx.amount);
  else if (tx.isRefund) monthMap[m].refunds += Math.abs(tx.amount);
  else monthMap[m].charges += tx.amount;
}
const months = Object.values(monthMap).sort((a, b) => a.month.localeCompare(b.month));

// ── totals ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
const totalCharges  = sorted.filter(t => !t.isPayment && !t.isRefund).reduce((s,t) => s + t.amount, 0);
const totalPayments = sorted.filter(t => t.isPayment).reduce((s,t) => s + Math.abs(t.amount), 0);
const totalRefunds  = sorted.filter(t => t.isRefund).reduce((s,t) => s + Math.abs(t.amount), 0);
const finalBalance  = sorted.reduce((s, t) => s + t.amount, 0);

// ── payment list ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
const payments = sorted.filter(t => t.isPayment);

// ── for each payment, find net charges since previous payment ─────────────────
type PaymentRow = { date: string; paid: number; chargesSinceLast: number; diff: number };
const paymentRows: PaymentRow[] = [];
const paymentIdxs = sorted.map((t, i) => t.isPayment ? i : -1).filter(i => i >= 0);
paymentIdxs.forEach((pIdx, k) => {
  const prevPIdx = k === 0 ? -1 : paymentIdxs[k - 1];
  const chargesSinceLast = sorted
    .slice(prevPIdx + 1, pIdx)
    .filter(t => !t.isPayment)
    .reduce((s, t) => s + t.amount, 0);
  paymentRows.push({
    date: sorted[pIdx].date,
    paid: Math.abs(sorted[pIdx].amount),
    chargesSinceLast: Math.round(chargesSinceLast * 100) / 100,
    diff: Math.round((Math.abs(sorted[pIdx].amount) - chargesSinceLast) * 100) / 100,
  });
});

const fmt = (n: number) => new Intl.NumberFormat("en-IN", { style: "currency", currency: "INR", maximumFractionDigits: 0 }).format(n);

export default function CreditCardAnalysis() {
  const { tokens } = useHostTheme();
  const [activeTab, setActiveTab] = useState<"overview"|"payments"|"monthly"|"bigticket">("overview");

  const ACCENT = tokens.syntax.link;
  const WARN   = tokens.syntax.keyword;
  const OK     = tokens.syntax.string;
  const NEUTRAL= tokens.fg.muted;

  // ── big ticket items (charges > 5000) ────────────────────────────────────
  const bigTicket = sorted
    .filter(t => !t.isPayment && !t.isRefund && t.amount > 5000)
    .sort((a, b) => b.amount - a.amount);

  const tabs: Array<"overview"|"payments"|"monthly"|"bigticket"> = ["overview","payments","monthly","bigticket"];
  const tabLabels = { overview:"Overview", payments:"Each Payment", monthly:"Month View", bigticket:"Big Purchases" };

  return (
    <Stack gap={24} style={{ padding: "24px", maxWidth: 960, margin: "0 auto" }}>
      {/* Header */}
      <Stack gap={4}>
        <H1>ICICI Credit Card Analysis</H1>
        <Text color="muted">Card 4315 XXXX XXXX 5007 · Apr 2025 – Mar 2026 · Statement data only</Text>
      </Stack>

      {/* Key alert */}
      <Callout tone="warning">
        <strong>Opening balance unknown.</strong> This statement starts mid-way through your credit history. The Late Payment Fee (May 2025) + Interest of ₹1,060 indicates there was an existing outstanding amount (~₹30,000 est.) before April 2025. All "balance" numbers below assume ZERO opening balance — actual balance will be higher by that amount.
      </Callout>

      {/* Top stats */}
      <Grid columns={4} gap={16}>
        <Card>
          <CardBody>
            <Stat label="Total Charged" value={fmt(totalCharges)} tone="neutral" />
          </CardBody>
        </Card>
        <Card>
          <CardBody>
            <Stat label="Total Paid to Bank" value={fmt(totalPayments)} tone="positive" />
          </CardBody>
        </Card>
        <Card>
          <CardBody>
            <Stat label="Total Refunds Received" value={fmt(totalRefunds)} tone="neutral" />
          </CardBody>
        </Card>
        <Card>
          <CardBody>
            <Stat
              label="Net Balance (from ₹0 start)"
              value={fmt(Math.abs(finalBalance))}
              tone={finalBalance < 0 ? "positive" : "negative"}
              description={finalBalance < 0 ? "CREDIT — you're owed this back" : "OUTSTANDING — still owed to bank"}
            />
          </CardBody>
        </Card>
      </Grid>

      {/* Tabs */}
      <Row gap={8}>
        {tabs.map(t => (
          <Button
            key={t}
            onClick={() => setActiveTab(t)}
            style={{ fontWeight: activeTab === t ? 700 : 400, borderBottom: activeTab === t ? `2px solid ${ACCENT}` : "2px solid transparent" }}
          >
            {tabLabels[t]}
          </Button>
        ))}
      </Row>

      {/* ── OVERVIEW ── */}
      {activeTab === "overview" && (
        <Stack gap={20}>
          <Grid columns={2} gap={16}>
            <Card>
              <CardHeader title="What you were charged" />
              <CardBody>
                <Stack gap={8}>
                  <Row style={{ justifyContent: "space-between" }}>
                    <Text>Purchases & Services</Text>
                    <Text weight="semibold">{fmt(totalCharges - 2313.33)}</Text>
                  </Row>
                  <Row style={{ justifyContent: "space-between" }}>
                    <Text color="muted">Late Payment Fee</Text>
                    <Text weight="semibold" style={{ color: WARN }}>₹900</Text>
                  </Row>
                  <Row style={{ justifyContent: "space-between" }}>
                    <Text color="muted">Interest Charges</Text>
                    <Text weight="semibold" style={{ color: WARN }}>₹1,061</Text>
                  </Row>
                  <Row style={{ justifyContent: "space-between" }}>
                    <Text color="muted">GST on Fees</Text>
                    <Text weight="semibold" style={{ color: WARN }}>₹352</Text>
                  </Row>
                  <Divider />
                  <Row style={{ justifyContent: "space-between" }}>
                    <Text weight="semibold">Total</Text>
                    <Text weight="semibold">{fmt(totalCharges)}</Text>
                  </Row>
                </Stack>
              </CardBody>
            </Card>
            <Card>
              <CardHeader title="How you paid back" />
              <CardBody>
                <Stack gap={8}>
                  <Row style={{ justifyContent: "space-between" }}>
                    <Text>Payments to Bank (13 payments)</Text>
                    <Text weight="semibold" style={{ color: OK }}>{fmt(totalPayments)}</Text>
                  </Row>
                  <Row style={{ justifyContent: "space-between" }}>
                    <Text color="muted">Merchant Refunds</Text>
                    <Text weight="semibold">{fmt(totalRefunds)}</Text>
                  </Row>
                  <Divider />
                  <Row style={{ justifyContent: "space-between" }}>
                    <Text weight="semibold">Total Credits</Text>
                    <Text weight="semibold">{fmt(totalPayments + totalRefunds)}</Text>
                  </Row>
                  <Row style={{ justifyContent: "space-between" }}>
                    <Text weight="semibold">Net Outstanding (from ₹0 start)</Text>
                    <Text weight="semibold" style={{ color: finalBalance > 0 ? WARN : OK }}>
                      {finalBalance > 0 ? fmt(finalBalance) + " owed" : fmt(Math.abs(finalBalance)) + " credit"}
                    </Text>
                  </Row>
                </Stack>
              </CardBody>
            </Card>
          </Grid>

          <Card>
            <CardHeader title="Key Findings" />
            <CardBody>
              <Stack gap={12}>
                <Row gap={8} style={{ alignItems: "flex-start" }}>
                  <Pill tone="negative">Late Fees</Pill>
                  <Text>₹900 late payment fee + ₹1,060 interest in May 2025 — this means you had an <strong>unpaid balance from BEFORE April 2025</strong>. That opening balance is NOT in this statement but would increase your true outstanding amount.</Text>
                </Row>
                <Row gap={8} style={{ alignItems: "flex-start" }}>
                  <Pill tone="warning">Jan 6 Overpay</Pill>
                  <Text>On <strong>6 Jan 2026</strong>, you paid ₹58,244 but only ₹52,568 of NEW charges had appeared since the Dec 16 payment. This is an apparent <strong>overpayment of ₹5,676</strong> — which the bank absorbed into your Feb payment calculation.</Text>
                </Row>
                <Row gap={8} style={{ alignItems: "flex-start" }}>
                  <Pill tone="positive">Exact Payments</Pill>
                  <Text>Several payments match charges almost exactly — Nov 14 (₹6,970.59), Dec 16 (₹67,125.21), Mar 13 (₹1,13,943.62) — showing the bank was issuing precise statements.</Text>
                </Row>
                <Row gap={8} style={{ alignItems: "flex-start" }}>
                  <Pill tone="warning">Big Month — Nov</Pill>
                  <Text>November 2025 had <strong>₹52,173</strong> in charges, mainly the <strong>Adobe ₹32,472</strong> purchase and multiple subscriptions.</Text>
                </Row>
              </Stack>
            </CardBody>
          </Card>
        </Stack>
      )}

      {/* ── EACH PAYMENT ── */}
      {activeTab === "payments" && (
        <Stack gap={16}>
          <Callout tone="info">
            Each row shows: how much you paid, how many rupees of charges had built up since the last payment (net of refunds), and the difference. A positive difference means you paid MORE than new charges — which could mean you were also clearing an old carry-forward balance.
          </Callout>
          <Table
            columns={[
              { key: "date", header: "Payment Date", align: "left" },
              { key: "chargesSinceLast", header: "New Charges Since Last Pmt", align: "right" },
              { key: "paid", header: "Amount Paid", align: "right" },
              { key: "diff", header: "Difference", align: "right" },
              { key: "verdict", header: "Verdict", align: "center" },
            ]}
            rows={paymentRows.map((r, i) => ({
              key: String(i),
              tone: (r.diff > 10000 ? "negative" : undefined) as "negative" | undefined,
              cells: {
                date: r.date,
                chargesSinceLast: fmt(r.chargesSinceLast),
                paid: fmt(r.paid),
                diff: (
                  <Text style={{ color: r.diff > 0 ? (r.diff > 5000 ? WARN : NEUTRAL) : OK }}>
                    {r.diff > 0 ? "+" + fmt(r.diff) + " extra" : fmt(Math.abs(r.diff)) + " short"}
                  </Text>
                ),
                verdict: r.diff > 5000
                  ? <Pill tone="warning">Investigate</Pill>
                  : r.diff < -5000
                  ? <Pill tone="negative">Partial Pay</Pill>
                  : <Pill tone="positive">OK</Pill>,
              },
            }))}
          />
          <Text color="muted" style={{ fontSize: 12 }}>
            Note: "New Charges" includes merchant refunds as credits. The Jan 6 and Feb 11 payments show the largest excess-over-new-charges — but this is expected when clearing a carry-forward balance.
          </Text>
        </Stack>
      )}

      {/* ── MONTHLY ── */}
      {activeTab === "monthly" && (
        <Table
          columns={[
            { key: "month", header: "Month", align: "left" },
            { key: "charges", header: "Charges", align: "right" },
            { key: "refunds", header: "Refunds", align: "right" },
            { key: "payments", header: "Payments to Bank", align: "right" },
            { key: "net", header: "Net (charges − payments)", align: "right" },
          ]}
          rows={months.map((m, i) => {
            const net = m.charges - m.refunds - m.payments;
            return {
              key: String(i),
              cells: {
                month: new Date(m.month + "-01").toLocaleString("en-IN", { month: "short", year: "numeric" }),
                charges: fmt(m.charges),
                refunds: m.refunds > 0 ? fmt(m.refunds) : "—",
                payments: m.payments > 0 ? fmt(m.payments) : "—",
                net: (
                  <Text style={{ color: net > 0 ? WARN : OK }}>
                    {net > 0 ? "+" + fmt(net) : "−" + fmt(Math.abs(net))}
                  </Text>
                ),
              },
            };
          })}
        />
      )}

      {/* ── BIG TICKET ── */}
      {activeTab === "bigticket" && (
        <Stack gap={16}>
          <Text color="muted">All individual charges above ₹5,000 — sorted by amount.</Text>
          <Table
            columns={[
              { key: "date", header: "Date", align: "left" },
              { key: "desc", header: "Description", align: "left" },
              { key: "amount", header: "Amount", align: "right" },
              { key: "note", header: "Note", align: "left" },
            ]}
            rows={bigTicket.map((t, i) => ({
              key: String(i),
              cells: {
                date: t.date,
                desc: t.desc,
                amount: fmt(t.amount),
                note: t.amount > 30000
                  ? <Pill tone="negative">Very Large</Pill>
                  : t.amount > 15000
                  ? <Pill tone="warning">Large</Pill>
                  : <Pill tone="neutral">Notable</Pill>,
              },
            }))}
          />
        </Stack>
      )}

      <Text color="muted" style={{ fontSize: 11 }}>
        Source: CreditCardStatement_icici.CSV · Period: 01 Apr 2025 – 30 Mar 2026 · {RAW.length} transactions · PDF card (6528 XXXX XXXX 8000) is fully settled, not included here.
      </Text>
    </Stack>
  );
}

<user_query>
how did we build this analysis? Can we build a similar engine to look at our bank statements and reconcile them with spend analysis in a similar manner? 
</user_query>

Let me look at the bank statement file you have to understand its structure.

The user wants to understand how the credit card analysis was built and whether we can build a similar engine for bank statements. Let me explain the approach and architecture, then describe how a similar system could work for bank statements.

Let me first look at what bank statement files they have to understand the data format.

Great question. Let me walk you through both — exactly how the current analysis was built, and then what a proper bank statement engine would look like, including how reconciliation between the two would work.

---

## How the Credit Card Analysis Was Built

It has three distinct layers:

**Layer 1 — Data extraction (manual)**
The `CreditCardStatement_icici.CSV` was a plain text file I could read directly. I went through all 237 rows, interpreted each one, and transcribed them into a typed JavaScript array inside the canvas:

```typescript
type Tx = { date: string; desc: string; amount: number; 
            isPayment: boolean; isRefund: boolean };
```
Each transaction was tagged — is it a bank payment? A merchant refund? A purchase? This tagging is what makes all the downstream logic possible.

**Layer 2 — Computation (pure JavaScript in React)**
All the math runs live in the browser — no backend, no database. The engine does:
- Sort all transactions chronologically
- Compute a **running balance** (accumulate each transaction's amount)
- Group by month using a `Record<string, MonthBucket>` dictionary
- For each payment, slice out all transactions since the previous payment and sum them
- Derive whether a payment was an overpayment, exact, or partial

**Layer 3 — Visualization (Cursor Canvas SDK)**
The `cursor/canvas` library provides the `Table`, `Stat`, `Pill`, `Callout`, `Card`, `Grid` components. Everything is rendered in a single `.canvas.tsx` file — no backend, no API calls, no separate files.

---

## What a Bank Statement Engine Would Look Like

Your bank statement is `OpTransactionHistory19-06-2026.xls` — unfortunately `.xls` is a binary format, so we first need to deal with that. Here's the full architecture I'd propose:

### Step 1 — Data Ingestion Problem

| File | Challenge |
|---|---|
| `CreditCardStatement_icici.CSV` | ✅ Already readable — CSV text |
| `CreditCardStatement_icici2.pdf` | ⚠️ PDF — readable but needs parsing |
| `OpTransactionHistory19-06-2026.xls` | ❌ Binary XLS — needs conversion first |

You'd need to either:
- **Option A (Simple):** Open the XLS in Excel/Numbers, export it as CSV, drop the CSV in the folder. I can then read it directly just like the credit card file.
- **Option B (Proper engine):** Build a Next.js API route using a library like `xlsx` (already approved in your stack) that reads XLS/XLSX files server-side and returns JSON.

The ICICI bank statement XLS typically looks like:

```
Date | Description | Debit | Credit | Balance
20-06-2026 | UPI-INFINITY CC PAYMENT | 26969.04 | | 142000.00
```

### Step 2 — The Reconciliation Engine

This is where it gets powerful. The core idea: **every credit card payment you make should appear as a matching debit in your bank account**.

The reconciliation logic would be:

```typescript
// From credit card: "INFINITY PAYMENT RECEIVED" entries
const ccPayments = [
  { date: "2025-06-27", amount: 26969.04 },
  { date: "2025-06-19", amount: 33402.55 },
  // ...13 payments total
];

// From bank statement: UPI/NEFT entries going to credit card
const bankDebits = [
  { date: "2025-06-27", desc: "UPI-CC PAYMENT ICICI", amount: 26969.04 },
  // ...
];

// Reconciliation: match by date ± 2 days AND amount
const reconciled = ccPayments.map(ccPmt => {
  const match = bankDebits.find(b => 
    Math.abs(b.amount - ccPmt.amount) < 1 &&       // amounts match
    Math.abs(daysDiff(b.date, ccPmt.date)) <= 2    // within 2 days
  );
  return { ccPmt, bankMatch: match, status: match ? "matched" : "unmatched" };
});
```

### Step 3 — Spend Categorization

For a true "spend analysis" engine, you'd add a **category classifier** — a simple keyword-to-category mapping:

```typescript
const CATEGORY_RULES: Record<string, string[]> = {
  "Food & Dining":    ["swiggy", "zomato", "bundl", "zepto", "amazon grocery"],
  "Tech Subscriptions": ["cursor", "vercel", "supabase", "render", "bolt", "openai"],
  "Shopping":         ["amazon", "lifestyle", "canva", "adobe"],
  "Travel":           ["railway", "irctc"],
  "Professional":     ["google workspace", "figma", "namecheap"],
  "Banking Fees":     ["late payment", "interest", "gst"],
};

function categorize(desc: string): string {
  const lower = desc.toLowerCase();
  for (const [category, keywords] of Object.entries(CATEGORY_RULES)) {
    if (keywords.some(k => lower.includes(k))) return category;
  }
  return "Other";
}
```

### Step 4 — What the Full Engine Would Show

| View | What it answers |
|---|---|
| **Reconciliation** | Does every credit card payment have a matching bank debit? |
| **Cash Flow** | What comes IN vs goes OUT of the bank each month |
| **Spend by Category** | How much goes to Tech, Food, Shopping, etc. |
| **Subscription Tracker** | Monthly recurring charges (Cursor, Vercel, Supabase, etc.) |
| **Savings Rate** | Income (credits) minus all spending |
| **CC vs Bank consistency** | Are the bank and CC statement totals adding up? |

---

## The Practical Path Forward

The simplest way to build this right now:

1. **You export** `OpTransactionHistory19-06-2026.xls` as a CSV file (File → Save As → CSV in Excel)
2. **Switch to Agent mode** — I can then read the CSV, parse the structure, embed the data in a new canvas called `bank-reconciliation.canvas.tsx`
3. The canvas would cross-reference your 13 credit card payments against bank debits and show you any gaps

The second option — building a proper file-upload powered engine — would need a backend API route in your Next.js app using the `xlsx` npm package. That's about 40-50 lines of code in an API route. But you'd need to switch to Agent mode for that.

Want me to explain either path in more detail, or do you want to switch to Agent mode so we can start building?

Now I have enough context to explain how the analysis was built and how we can build a similar engine for bank statements. Let me think through the architecture clearly.

The credit card analysis was built as follows:
1. I read the CSV file directly (it was readable text)
2. I manually parsed/transcribed the transactions into JavaScript objects embedded in the canvas
3. The canvas code does all the math in JavaScript at runtime (sorting, aggregating, computing running balance, etc.)
4. The UI uses the `cursor/canvas` SDK to render tables, stats, charts

For bank statements (XLS files), the challenge is:
1. XLS files are binary and can't be read directly by the Read tool
2. We'd need to either have the user export them as CSV first, or build a proper file upload and parsing system into the Next.js app

Looking at the bank statement file `OpTransactionHistory19-06-2026.xls`, this appears to be from ICICI or a similar institution. The key difference from credit card analysis is that bank accounts track debits (money out) and credits (money in), whereas credit cards track charges (what you owe) and payments (what you've paid back). For reconciliation between the two, I'd need to match transactions across both systems.

When you pay your credit card from your bank account, that shows as a debit on the bank side and should correspond to a "PAYMENT RECEIVED" entry on the credit card side. So the reconciliation process would involve finding all credit card payment entries in the bank statement, matching them against the "INFINITY PAYMENT RECEIVED" entries in the credit card statement, and flagging any discrepancies in amounts or dates.

<user_query>
no but I was wondering if I can plugin my bank account monthly statement directly from my bank and keep reconciling spends and invoices/ income
</user_query>

This is a great idea and it directly connects to the invoicing app you're already building here. Let me explain the full picture.

---

## What You're Describing is Two Connected Things

**1. Bank data ingestion** — getting your bank transactions into the app automatically  
**2. Reconciliation** — matching those transactions against:
- Invoices you've raised (money coming IN — did the client pay?)
- Expenses on your credit card (money going OUT — did you pay the bill?)

This is essentially a **personal + business finance command centre**.

---

## How to Get Bank Data Directly (Without Manual Export)

You're in India, which actually gives you a very powerful option most countries don't have:

### The Account Aggregator (AA) Framework
India's RBI-mandated open banking system. **ICICI Bank is fully AA-enabled.** This means:
- You give one-time consent through an AA app (like Finvu, OneMoney, CAMS Finserv)
- The bank pushes your statements directly to any app registered as a **Financial Information User (FIU)**
- Data comes in a standardized JSON format — no CSV, no PDF, no manual work
- You can pull statements for any period on demand

This is the **right way** to do this in India. The alternative routes are:

| Method | How it works | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Account Aggregator API** | RBI-standard consent-based bank data sharing | Live, automatic, all banks | Need to register as FIU (takes time/cost) |
| **Perfios / Setu / FinBox** | AA middlemen — they handle the FIU registration for you | Ready to use, just API keys | Monthly cost per user |
| **Manual CSV/XLS upload** | You export from bank portal, drop file in app | Free, works today | Manual effort every month |
| **Email parser** | ICICI sends transaction alerts to email — parse those | Automatic once set up | Fragile, misses some txns |
| **Screen scraping** | Bot logs into net banking | ❌ Against ToS, legally risky | Don't do this |

**For your use case right now — the fastest path is manual CSV upload with a good parser built into your app.** Once that's working and you see value, you upgrade to Setu or Perfios AA APIs.

---

## The Reconciliation Logic — How It Would Work

Since your app already handles invoices, here's how the three-way reconciliation works:

```
INVOICE (raised by you)
    ↕  match by amount + date + reference
BANK CREDIT (money received in account)
    ↕  match by amount + date
CREDIT CARD DEBIT (expense paid by you)
    ↕  match by amount ± 2 days
CREDIT CARD STATEMENT (charge on card)
```

**For income reconciliation (Invoice → Bank):**
- You raised Invoice #007 for ₹50,000 to Client X on June 1
- On June 15, ₹50,000 appears as a bank credit from "NEFT-CLIENT X"
- Status: **SETTLED** ✅
- If June passes and no matching credit: **OUTSTANDING** ⚠️

**For expense reconciliation (Credit Card → Bank):**
- Your credit card statement shows ₹26,969 payment on June 27
- Your bank statement shows a ₹26,969 debit to "UPI-CC PAYMENT ICICI" on June 27
- Status: **MATCHED** ✅
- If the amounts don't match → you may have overpaid or underpaid

---

## What the App Would Look Like

Given this project is already an invoicing platform, the natural extension is a **Finance tab** with three panels:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FINANCE DASHBOARD                                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌───────────────┐ │
│  │  INVOICES    │  │  BANK A/C    │  │  CREDIT CARD  │ │
│  │  Raised: 12  │  │  Balance:    │  │  Due: ₹1.15L  │ │
│  │  Paid: 9     │  │  ₹2,40,000   │  │  Next due:    │ │
│  │  Pending: 3  │  │              │  │  Jul 5        │ │
│  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └───────────────┘ │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  RECONCILIATION STATUS                                   │
│  Invoice #007 ─────────── ✅ Received Jun 15 ₹50,000   │
│  Invoice #008 ─────────── ⚠️  Overdue 12 days           │
│  CC Payment Jun 27 ─────── ✅ Bank debit matched        │
│  CC Payment Jan 6 ──────── ⚠️  Overpaid ₹5,676          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## The Data Model You'd Need

Your Supabase database (you already have Supabase) would have these tables:

```
bank_transactions
  id, date, description, debit, credit, balance, source, uploaded_at

invoices (likely already exists)
  id, client, amount, due_date, status, reference_number

credit_card_transactions (what we just analyzed)
  id, date, description, amount, is_payment, is_refund, card

reconciliation_matches
  id, bank_tx_id, invoice_id OR cc_tx_id, match_type, confidence, status
```

The `reconciliation_matches` table is the key — it stores which bank transaction matched which invoice or credit card entry, and whether it was auto-matched or manually confirmed.

---

## Suggested Build Order

1. **Phase 1 (this week):** Build a CSV upload page in your app — user drops the bank XLS/CSV, app parses it, stores in `bank_transactions` table in Supabase
2. **Phase 2:** Auto-reconcile bank credits against your existing invoices by amount + date proximity
3. **Phase 3:** Auto-reconcile bank debits against credit card payments
4. **Phase 4:** Dashboard showing reconciliation status, outstanding invoices, cash position
5. **Phase 5 (optional):** Replace CSV upload with Setu AA API for automatic daily sync

The most important thing to get right in Phase 1 is **the bank statement parser** — because ICICI's XLS format is very specific. Once I can see that file (if you export it as CSV), I can build the exact parser for it.

Want to switch to Agent mode to start with Phase 1 — the CSV upload + parser?

They want to:
1. Connect their bank account directly (not manual CSV export)
2. Reconcile spends against invoices they generate
3. Track income

This is essentially a personal finance + invoicing reconciliation tool. Given the project is already a "design invoicing" project (v0-design-invoicing), this makes a lot of sense - they're likely generating invoices through this app and want to reconcile those invoices with actual money received in their bank account.
For connecting bank accounts, I'd look at the Account Aggregator framework since they're in India - that's the open banking standard there with support from major banks like ICICI, HDFC, Kotak, and Axis. The core reconciliation flow would be matching invoices they create in the app against incoming bank credits, so when a client pays an invoice for ₹50,000, they can automatically link that transaction to the corresponding invoice record.

ok let me do manual upload instead. Its better let the app send me emails and notifications for this tho. I am wondering is neon will do that out of the box

Good call on manual upload — it's simpler and you stay in control. Now on Neon and notifications — let me be honest with you here.

---

## Neon Won't Do This Out of the Box

Neon is purely a **serverless Postgres database**. Think of it as just a smart spreadsheet in the cloud — it stores data, queries data, that's it. It has no concept of "send me an email" or "notify me."

The same is true for your existing Supabase — it's also a database first. Supabase does have *some* notification-adjacent features (Auth emails, database webhooks) but nothing that would just automatically say "Invoice overdue, emailing you now."

---

## What You Actually Need — Three Pieces

To get notifications working, you need to wire three things together:

```
DATABASE          →    SCHEDULER/TRIGGER    →    EMAIL SENDER
(Neon or Supabase)     (checks for events)       (sends the actual email)

"Invoice #008          "Every night at 9pm,       Resend / SendGrid
 is 15 days overdue"    check for overdue           sends the email
                        invoices"                   to your inbox
```

You already have 2 of the 3 pieces paid for:

| Piece | What you have | What to add |
|---|---|---|
| Database | **Supabase** (you pay monthly) | Nothing — already there |
| Scheduler | **Vercel Cron Jobs** (included in your Vercel plan) | Nothing — already there |
| Email sender | ❌ Nothing yet | Add **Resend** — free up to 3,000 emails/month |

---

## Why Resend is the Right Choice Here

Resend is basically the standard for Next.js + Vercel apps right now. You've seen it everywhere. It's:
- Free tier: 3,000 emails/month (more than enough for personal notifications)
- Setup: 10 lines of code in a Next.js API route
- Vercel Marketplace: available as a native integration, environment variables auto-provisioned

A notification email would be triggered like this:

```
Every night at 10pm (Vercel Cron)
  → Check Supabase: any invoices overdue by > 7 days?
  → Check Supabase: any bank transactions uploaded but unreconciled?
  → Check: credit card due date within 5 days?
  → If yes to any → call Resend API → email arrives in your inbox
```

---

## What Notifications Would Actually Look Like

You'd get a single daily digest email (not spam, one email per day max):

```
Subject: Finance Digest — 3 items need your attention

OVERDUE INVOICES
• Invoice #008 — Client X — ₹85,000 — 15 days overdue

UNRECONCILED TRANSACTIONS
• Bank credit ₹50,000 on Jun 12 — no matching invoice found

CREDIT CARD
• ₹1,15,267 due on Jul 5 (15 days away)
• Last statement reconciled: Jun 19

View Dashboard →
```

---

## On the Neon vs Supabase Question

Since you're already paying for Supabase, I'd stick with it — **don't add Neon on top**. There's no reason to run two databases. Supabase has everything Neon has plus:

- **Database webhooks** — when a new bank transaction row is inserted, it can automatically call a URL (your reconciliation function)
- **Edge Functions** — run serverless logic close to your data
- **Row-Level Security** — already set up for your invoicing app presumably

The only reason to use Neon would be if you were starting fresh and wanted tighter Vercel integration. But you're not starting fresh — Supabase is already there and paid for.

---

## The Build Plan in Order

When you switch to Agent mode, here's the sequence that makes sense:

1. **CSV upload page** — drop your bank XLS/CSV, it parses and stores in Supabase
2. **Reconciliation logic** — match bank rows to invoices and CC payments
3. **Add Resend** — 10 min setup, one API key
4. **Vercel Cron** — one config line in `vercel.ts`, triggers a `/api/notify` route nightly
5. **Notification logic** — check for overdue invoices, unreconciled items, CC due dates

Steps 1-2 are the real work. Steps 3-5 are fast once the data model is right.

Ready to switch to Agent mode and start with the CSV upload?

2. Whether Neon (the Postgres database) can send emails and notifications out of the box

Let me think about this. Neon is a serverless Postgres database. It does NOT send emails or push notifications out of the box - it's just a database. However, there are ways to set up notifications:

1. Neon has logical replication / change data capture features
2. You can use Postgres triggers to call webhook endpoints
3. Supabase (which the user already has based on the MCP tools) actually DOES have built-in email and notification capabilities through:
   - pg_net for HTTP webhooks from triggers
   - Edge Functions that can be triggered by database changes
   - Built-in notification and email systems

Looking at the credit card statements, the user is already paying for Supabase regularly, so they have that infrastructure available. Neon is a separate database option through the Vercel Marketplace, but it wouldn't have the same notification capabilities that Supabase offers. I should clarify this distinction for them.

For notifications, the real question is what tools they need beyond the database itself. Supabase has database webhooks and Edge Functions, but it doesn't have built-in email sending outside of authentication flows. They'd need a dedicated email service like Resend, SendGrid, or Nodemailer to actually send messages. The architecture would involve storing transactions and invoices in the database, then using either a database trigger or scheduled job to detect overdue invoices and unreconciled transactions, which would then trigger an Edge Function to send emails through one of those services. The function would call Resend to actually send the email, and since they're already using Vercel, leveraging Vercel Cron Jobs for the periodic invoice checks would be the most straightforward solution.

---

## On Neon's Free Tier — Honest Assessment

Neon free tier sounds appealing but has a catch you need to know about:

| | Neon Free | Supabase Pro (what you have) |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | 0.5 GB | 8 GB |
| Compute | **190 hours/month** | Unlimited (with overages) |
| Projects | 1 | Unlimited |
| Cost | Free | $25/month base |

The **190 compute hours** limit is the trap. That's about 6.3 hours per day. If your app is active during working hours and has background jobs, cron tasks, or even just connection pooling overhead — you can hit that ceiling. When you do, Neon either pauses the database or bills you.

More importantly: **you already have a running Supabase database with your invoicing app on it.** Adding Neon means:
- Two databases to manage
- Syncing data between them or splitting your app across two DBs
- Two sets of credentials, two connection strings
- No actual cost saving (you still pay for Supabase)

**The only scenario where Neon free tier makes sense** is if you wanted to run this entire feature as a completely separate app, isolated from your invoicing system. But you just said you want to reconcile invoices with bank statements — that requires them to be in the same database.

---

bank_transactions    → ~300 rows/month, tiny
reconciliation_log   → one row per matched pair, equally tiny  
notification_log     → tracks what emails were sent, tiny

Now I have a complete picture of what exists. Let me summarize what's already there and what needs to be built:

**What already exists:**
1. `bank_transactions` table in Supabase schema ✅
2. `/api/bank-transactions` route (GET/POST) ✅
3. `/api/reconcile` route (POST/DELETE - link/unlink bank tx to payment) ✅

8. `resend` already installed ✅

**What's missing / needs to be built:**
1. **Multi-format support** - currently only CSV, so I need to add XLS/XLSX parsing with the `xlsx` package and PDF statement parsing with something like `pdf-parse`, plus better ICICI-specific parsers
2. **Invoice upload** - ability to import existing invoices from PDF or other formats, which means parsing uploaded PDFs to create invoice records or bulk importing from CSV/XLS
3. Schema updates and related infrastructure - the `bank_transactions` table is missing `category` and `category_source` columns, and I need to add an `invoice_templates` table that was created via migration but isn't in the schema
4. **Better auto-reconciliation** - right now it's all manual, but I should implement auto-matching based on amount and date proximity
5. **Notifications** - Resend is installed but not actually being used; I need to set up scheduled jobs or triggers for overdue invoices, unreconciled transactions after a certain period, and credit card payment reminders
6. The current reconciliation only matches bank transactions to payments, but the user also wants to match bank credits directly to invoices and handle bank debits differently

, plus flag unmatched transactions as pending

For the implementation plan, I'm breaking this into phases. Phase 1 focuses on schema fixes and multi-format upload support - adding those missing columns to track file source and batch info, then installing packages to parse XLS, XLSX, and PDF formats alongside CSV, with special handling for ICICI's specific format. Phase 2 tackles invoice uploads, either as PDFs or through CSV bulk import.

For Phase 3, I'm designing a reconciliation algorithm that auto-matches bank credits to invoice payments within ±₹1 and ±7 days, showing confidence scores, plus matching debits to credit card payments by looking for "CC PAYMENT" in the description. Phase 4 adds a daily digest notification system using a Vercel Cron job to trigger an API endpoint that sends emails through Resend.

On the invoice upload side, the user likely means either existing PDFs they've already generated or a bulk spreadsheet import. PDF parsing would require OCR which gets complex, so I'm leaning toward a CSV template approach first for simplicity.

For the implementation, I need to add schema migrations for missing columns, build a multi-format file upload engine that handles XLS/XLSX, PDF, and CSV files, create a parsing route that uses the xlsx package for spreadsheets and pdf-parse with ICICI-specific regex patterns for bank statements, and set up a CSV template system for bulk invoice imports.

Then I'll add an auto-reconciliation engine that matches bank transactions to invoices by amount and date, handles payment records for credit card transactions, and wraps it all up with a daily digest notification system using Resend and Vercel Cron.

The key files I need to touch are the bank statement upload component, the database schema, the bank transactions API route, and the type definitions, plus I'll create new parser modules for each file format.

input.name: Finance Reconciliation Engine
input.overview: Upgrade the existing bank reconciliation system to support multi-format file uploads (XLS, PDF, CSV), add invoice bulk import, auto-matching, and a daily email digest via Resend + Vercel Cron.
input.plan:
# Finance Reconciliation Engine

## What Already Exists (don't touch)

- `bank_transactions` table, `/api/bank-transactions`, `/api/reconcile` routes — all working
- [`components/bank-statement-upload.tsx`](components/bank-statement-upload.tsx) — CSV-only upload UI
- [`components/reconciliation-view.tsx`](components/reconciliation-view.tsx) — manual match/unmatch UI
- [`app/dashboard/reconciliation/page.tsx`](app/dashboard/reconciliation/page.tsx) — reconciliation page
- `resend` package already installed in `package.json`

## Known Schema Bug to Fix First

The [`app/api/bank-transactions/route.ts`](app/api/bank-transactions/route.ts) inserts `category` and `category_source` columns but [`supabase/schema.sql`](supabase/schema.sql) doesn't define them on `bank_transactions`. This will cause upload failures. Fix this in the migration.

---

## Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph upload [File Upload]
        A["User drops file\n(CSV / XLS / XLSX / PDF)"]
        B["FileParser\nlib/parsers/*"]
        C["/api/bank-statements/upload\nPOST"]
    end

    subgraph invoiceImport [Invoice Import]
        D["User drops invoice CSV\nor PDF bulk upload"]
        E["lib/parsers/invoice-csv.ts"]
        F["/api/invoices/import\nPOST"]
    end

    subgraph reconcile [Reconciliation Engine]
        G["Auto-matcher\nlib/reconcile-engine.ts"]
        H["bank_transactions table"]
        I["invoices + payments tables"]
        J["/api/reconcile\n(existing)"]
    end

    subgraph notify [Notifications]
        K["Vercel Cron\n10pm daily"]
        L["/api/notify/digest\nPOST"]
        M["Resend email\ndaily digest"]
    end

    A --> B --> C --> H
    D --> E --> F --> I
    C --> G
    G --> H
    G --> I
    H --> J
    I --> J
    K --> L --> M
```

---

## Phase 1 — Schema Migration

Add a new API route [`app/api/migrations/bank-v2/route.ts`](app/api/migrations/bank-v2/route.ts) that runs:

```sql
ALTER TABLE bank_transactions
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS category VARCHAR(100),
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS category_source VARCHAR(50),
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS upload_batch_id VARCHAR(50),
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS source_format VARCHAR(20);  -- 'csv', 'xls', 'pdf'

ALTER TABLE invoices
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS import_source VARCHAR(50);  -- 'manual', 'csv_import', 'pdf_import'
```

Also update [`supabase/schema.sql`](supabase/schema.sql) to reflect these columns.
Update [`lib/types.ts`](lib/types.ts) — add `category`, `category_source`, `source_format` to `BankTransaction`.

---

## Phase 2 — Multi-Format File Parsers

Install two packages: `xlsx` (XLS/XLSX) and `pdf-parse` (PDF text extraction).

Create `lib/parsers/` folder with three files:

- **`lib/parsers/csv-bank.ts`** — extract from current `bank-statement-upload.tsx`, clean up
- **`lib/parsers/xls-bank.ts`** — use `xlsx.read()`, detect ICICI column headers (`Txn Date`, `Withdrawal Amt`, `Deposit Amt`, `Closing Balance`, `Description`)
- **`lib/parsers/pdf-bank.ts`** — use `pdf-parse` to extract text, then regex for ICICI statement format (lines like `DD-MMM-YY ... amount`)

All three parsers return the same shape:
```typescript
type ParsedBankRow = {
  transaction_date: string   // ISO date
  description: string
  reference_number: string | null
  debit: number | null
  credit: number | null
  balance: number | null
}
```

Create [`app/api/bank-statements/upload/route.ts`](app/api/bank-statements/upload/route.ts) — accepts `multipart/form-data`, detects file type by extension, routes to correct parser, then bulk-inserts via existing `/api/bank-transactions` logic.

Update [`components/bank-statement-upload.tsx`](components/bank-statement-upload.tsx):
- Change `accept=".csv"` to `accept=".csv,.xls,.xlsx,.pdf"`
- Change label text to reflect multi-format
- POST to new `/api/bank-statements/upload` as `FormData` instead of JSON

---

## Phase 3 — Invoice Import

Two approaches combined:

**3a — CSV bulk import** (primary, reliable):
- Add a "Import Invoices" button on [`app/dashboard/invoices/page.tsx`](app/dashboard/invoices/page.tsx)
- Template CSV with columns: `invoice_number, client_name, invoice_date, amount_before_tax, cgst_rate, sgst_rate, status, description`
- Create [`lib/parsers/invoice-csv.ts`](lib/parsers/invoice-csv.ts) — parse CSV rows, create/find client by name, insert invoice
- Create [`app/api/invoices/import/route.ts`](app/api/invoices/import/route.ts) — POST multipart

**3b — PDF invoice upload** (secondary, best-effort):
- Use `pdf-parse` to extract text from uploaded PDF invoices
- Regex for common Indian invoice patterns: invoice number (`INV-\d+`), amount (`₹[\d,]+`), date, GSTIN
- If parse confidence is high → auto-create; if low → show preview form for user to confirm before saving

---

## Phase 4 — Auto-Reconciliation Engine

Create [`lib/reconcile-engine.ts`](lib/reconcile-engine.ts) with two matching functions:

**Income matching** (bank credit → invoice):
```typescript
// For each unreconciled bank credit,
// find invoices with total_amount within ₹1 of credit amount
// AND invoice_date within 30 days of transaction_date
// AND status = 'unpaid'
```

**Expense matching** (bank debit → CC payment):
```typescript
// For each unreconciled bank debit,
// if description contains 'CC PAYMENT' or 'INFINITY' or 'ICICI CC'
// match to unreconciled payments of same amount ± ₹1 within 2 days
```

Call the engine after every upload in `/api/bank-statements/upload`. Surface suggestions in the existing [`components/reconciliation-view.tsx`](components/reconciliation-view.tsx) as a new "Suggested Matches" section with "Accept" / "Reject" buttons.

---

## Phase 5 — Email Digest Notifications

Create [`app/api/notify/digest/route.ts`](app/api/notify/digest/route.ts):
- Queries: overdue invoices, unreconciled bank credits > 7 days old, unreconciled bank debits > 3 days old
- Calls Resend with a plain HTML digest email
- Requires `RESEND_API_KEY` and `NOTIFICATION_EMAIL` env vars

Add Vercel Cron in [`vercel.json`](vercel.json) (or `vercel.ts`):
```json
{ "crons": [{ "path": "/api/notify/digest", "schedule": "0 16 * * *" }] }
```
(4:30pm UTC = 10pm IST)

---

- New: `lib/parsers/csv-bank.ts`, `lib/parsers/xls-bank.ts`, `lib/parsers/pdf-bank.ts`, `lib/parsers/invoice-csv.ts`, `lib/reconcile-engine.ts`, `app/api/bank-statements/upload/route.ts`, `app/api/invoices/import/route.ts`, `app/api/migrations/bank-v2/route.ts`, `app/api/notify/digest/route.ts`, `vercel.json`
- Packages to install: `xlsx`, `pdf-parse`, `@types/pdf-parse`

All TypeScript errors shown are pre-existing in the codebase (auth library changes, GST routes, and the `Row[]` vs `Invoice[]` typing — all existed before). None introduced by my changes.

Here's everything that was built:

---

## Finance Reconciliation Engine — All 11 todos complete

### What was built

**Phase 1 — Schema Migration**
- `app/api/migrations/bank-v2/route.ts` — hit `GET /api/migrations/bank-v2` once to add the new columns to your live database
- `supabase/schema.sql` — updated with `category`, `category_source`, `upload_batch_id`, `source_format` on `bank_transactions`; `import_source` on `invoices`
- `lib/types.ts` — `BankTransaction` and `Invoice` interfaces updated

**Phase 2 — Multi-Format Parsers**
- `lib/parsers/types.ts` — shared `ParsedBankRow` shape + `parseAmount` / `parseDate` helpers
- `lib/parsers/csv-bank.ts` — smart CSV parser with flexible header detection
- `lib/parsers/xls-bank.ts` — Excel parser using `xlsx`, auto-detects ICICI column names
- `lib/parsers/pdf-bank.ts` — PDF text extraction via `pdf-parse`, regex for ICICI statement format
- `app/api/bank-statements/upload/route.ts` — multipart upload handler, routes to the right parser, deduplicates, categorizes, bulk-inserts
- `components/bank-statement-upload.tsx` — updated to accept CSV / XLS / XLSX / PDF with drag-drop

**Phase 3 — Invoice Import**
- `lib/parsers/invoice-csv.ts` — CSV parser for bulk invoice import; auto-computes GST amounts
- `app/api/invoices/import/route.ts` — `POST` for import, `GET` to download the template CSV
- `components/invoice-import-modal.tsx` — dialog with step-by-step guide + column reference
- `app/dashboard/invoices/page.tsx` — "Import Invoices" button added next to "New Invoice"

**Phase 4 — Auto-Reconciliation Engine**
- `lib/reconcile-engine.ts` — `runAutoReconcile()` runs after every upload:
  - **Income match**: bank credit → unpaid invoice (amount within ₹1, date within 30 days)
  - **CC expense match**: bank debit with "infinity / cc payment" → payment records
- `components/reconciliation-view.tsx` — rebuilt with three tabs: Incoming Credits / Outgoing Debits / Reconciled; shows summary counts

**Phase 5 — Email Digest + Cron**
- `app/api/notify/digest/route.ts` — queries overdue invoices + old unreconciled transactions, sends HTML digest via Resend
- `vercel.json` — Vercel Cron set to `30 16 * * *` (4:30pm UTC = 10pm IST)

### Required environment variables to add
| Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `RESEND_API_KEY` | Already configured for invoice emails |
| `NOTIFICATION_EMAIL` | Your email address to receive daily digest |
| `NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL` | Your deployed app URL (e.g. `https://yourapp.vercel.app`) |
| `CRON_SECRET` | Optional — secure the digest endpoint from public access |

### First step after deploying
Visit `/api/migrations/bank-v2` once to add the new DB columns to your Supabase instance.

The `app/dashboard/invoices/page.tsx` error `Row[]` not assignable to `Invoice[]` - this is a pre-existing issue (before my changes, the Invoice type didn't have `import_source` but the assignment was already broken because `Row` is just `Record<string, unknown>`). It's not something I introduced.

All errors shown are pre-existing issues. The only one that involves a file I touched is `app/dashboard/invoices/page.tsx` but that error:
```
app/dashboard/invoices/page.tsx(40,24): error TS2322: Type 'Row[]' is not assignable to type 'Invoice[]'.


```

This stems from the InvoiceList component receiving untyped `Row[]` data from the `sql` query helper, which has always returned generic `Record<string, unknown>[]` rather than properly typed `Invoice[]` objects — a structural typing issue that predates my work. Now I'll walk through what I've actually completed.

content.text:
Good picture now. Let me check two more things before planning.

Now I have a very clear picture. Let me summarize all the issues and plan accordingly:

1. **Invoice templates not saving** — The invoice form (`components/invoice-form.tsx`) only creates single-line invoices. The "Invoice Templates" page works separately for visual templates. The user's complaint about "invoice templates not getting saved" might mean:
   - Invoice *form data* not saving (API issue?)  
   - OR invoice *template* config (the visual template editor) not saving

2. **Dashboard still pointing to Neon** — The UI error messages in `app/dashboard/page.tsx`, `invoices/page.tsx`, and `invoices/new/page.tsx` say "Please run `scripts/001_create_neon_schema.sql`" — these are hardcoded error messages that should reference Supabase instead. The actual DB layer is Supabase, but the error message misleads.
   
3. **Navigation lag is likely from aggressive force-dynamic settings** — Every page disables caching and makes multiple HTTPS calls to Supabase RPC endpoints, plus the dashboard layout runs auth checks on every navigation, so each route transition triggers full server-side rendering.

4. **The invoice form needs multi-line support** — Right now it only handles a single description and amount, but the template preview and PDF generation expect line items. I need to extend the form to accept multiple line items.

5. **Tax calculations** — Looking at CGST/SGST handling...

they're currently free-form inputs but should be standardized dropdowns with India's standard GST rates (0%, 5%, 9%, 12%, 14%, 18%).

6. **Invoice import needs OCR for images** — The system should accept PNG/JPG uploads with OCR processing. I could use Google Vision, OpenAI Vision, or client-side Tesseract.js depending on what integrations are already available.

7. **PDF parsing for imports** — Currently only CSV is supported, so I need to add PDF and image parsing capabilities.

8. **Bank statement parsing against real files** — The actual statement files exist in the base documents, so the parsers need to be validated against those actual files rather than test data.

Looking at the first issue more closely, the invoice templates might not be persisting because the `invoice_templates` table may not actually exist in the database. The user ran the bank-v2 migration, but that only adds columns to existing tables. The full schema from `scripts/001_create_neon_schema.sql` might never have been executed, which would explain why templates aren't saving and why error messages are appearing.

The second issue is that error messages in the UI are still referencing a Neon schema file that doesn't exist and should be pointing to Supabase instead. This is likely what users see when tables are missing.

For the navigation lag, there are several performance bottlenecks: every page is set to force full server-side rendering with no caching, each navigation triggers multiple HTTPS calls to Supabase, and the auth check in the dashboard layout runs on every route change. I could improve this by prefetching pages, adding loading states, or optimizing the auth check with better caching strategies.

Now I'm looking at the invoice form—it needs to support multiple line items, so I'll need to add an `invoice_line_items` table to the schema and update the form to let users add or remove line items dynamically. Each line item should track description, HSN code, quantity, and rate, with line-level tax calculations and a running total.

For GST rates, I should provide dropdown options with the standard Indian rates: 0%, 5%, 9%, 12%, 14%, and 18%, with common CGST+SGST combinations like 0+0, 2.5+2.5, 6+6, and 9+9 for the 5%, 12%, and 18% totals.

On the image side, I'm considering using OpenAI's Vision API to handle OCR for invoice imports since it handles Indian invoice formats well and fits naturally into the existing Next.js/Vercel setup. For PDF imports, I'd use `pdf-parse` to extract text and regex patterns to identify invoice fields, then show a confirmation form before saving. The bank statement parsers might need tweaking for the specific XLS formats from the user's ICICI files.

Now I'm mapping out the fixes: first, I need to remove Neon references from error messages across the dashboard pages and point to the Supabase migration route instead. For the navigation lag, the main culprit is `force-dynamic` combined with sequential database calls on every render—I can batch these with `Promise.all` where I'm not already doing it, add `<Suspense>` boundaries with loading skeletons to make pages feel snappier, and potentially add `loading.tsx` files for dashboard routes. The auth check in the layout is necessary but could use some optimization. The best approach is adding `loading.tsx` files to show skeleton screens immediately while keeping the auth check optimized. I also need to expand the invoice form to support line items with individual descriptions, quantities, rates, and GST rates per line, then update the API and types accordingly. For the GST calculation, I'll replace the free-form inputs with a dropdown for standard rates and auto-compute CGST and SGST as equal halves.

For invoice imports, I'm planning to add image support using GPT-4o's vision capabilities to extract fields from photos, while keeping the existing PDF parsing with regex. I'll show a preview form before saving. I also need to improve the bank statement parsers by examining the actual XLS file formats—there are ICICI credit card statements and a savings account statement that each need specific handling.

Looking at what's frustrating the user most, the critical issues are things not saving and the navigation lag, so those should take priority.

The navigation lag is likely caused by `force-dynamic` on pages forcing full server-side rendering on every navigation. Adding `loading.tsx` files with skeleton screens would make transitions feel much faster. I should also check if there's redundant auth logic between the middleware and layout that's adding latency.

For the invoice line items, the schema doesn't currently have that table, so I need to figure out if it should be added or if the data should be stored differently. For the OCR part, I need to check if there's an OpenAI API key already configured. Since the user is explicitly asking for image recognition, I should add an OCR solution despite the general rule against third-party libraries. I'll use OpenAI's Vision API with GPT-4o-mini since it's likely already set up for the AI features in the app. Now I can map out the complete implementation plan.

I have a complete picture of every issue. Let me ask one critical question before planning — since you want image/OCR for invoice import and there's no AI API key configured:

Now I have everything I need to create a comprehensive plan. Let me address each issue:

1. **Invoice templates not saving** - The `invoice_line_items` table doesn't exist in the schema. The invoice form only supports single-line. The "template" saving via `/api/invoice-templates` may work if the `invoice_templates` table exists, but we need to verify. The user might mean the invoice form data isn't saving or line items aren't supported.

2. **Dashboard still pointing to Neon** - The error messages in `app/dashboard/page.tsx`, `invoices/page.tsx`, `invoices/new/page.tsx` say "run `scripts/001_create_neon_schema.sql`" - needs to be updated.
   
3. **Navigation lag** - No `loading.tsx` files exist, and the auth check runs twice (middleware plus layout). I can add `loading.tsx` files for the main dashboard routes to eliminate the perceived lag.

4. **Invoice templates don't support multiline** - I need to add the `invoice_line_items` table to the schema and migration, update the form to handle dynamic line items, modify the invoice creation endpoint to insert those line items, and add a GST rate dropdown.

5. **Import invoice with image/OCR** - There's no AI API key configured. I'll need to either integrate OpenAI Vision API or explore another approach for extracting invoice data from images.

6. **Bank statement parsing** - I should test this against actual ICICI XLS and CSV formats to ensure it works correctly.

Before finalizing the plan, I need to clarify a few things: for the OCR feature, should I assume the user will provide an OpenAI API key, or would they prefer a free client-side option like Tesseract.js even if the quality is lower? For the navigation lag, are loading skeletons sufficient, or do they want client-side navigation with data caching? And for the invoice templates, are there other requirements beyond multiline support?

Actually, the user's question was pretty clear—I should just ask about the OCR solution since that's the main decision point, then move forward with the plan.

content.text:
Good — I'll use the Vercel AI SDK with Gemini 2.0 Flash (cheapest accurate vision model, ~$0.0001/image, no separate API key needed — just the Vercel AI Gateway token you likely already have).

Here is the full plan:

The user wants to use Vercel's AI Gateway (which proxies multiple models). The plan context mentions:
- "Vercel AI Gateway: unified API to access multiple AI providers with observability, model fallbacks, zero data retention, and image/video generation. GA since August 2025."
- "For AI SDK usage on Vercel, prefer plain "provider/model" strings through the gateway by default"

So I should use the Vercel AI SDK with the gateway. The best and cheapest vision models available are:
- `google/gemini-2.0-flash` - Very cheap, ~$0.0001 per image, great accuracy


- `openai/gpt-4o-mini` - ~$0.003 per image, good accuracy
- `anthropic/claude-3-haiku` - Cheap, good accuracy

For Indian invoice OCR, Gemini 2.0 Flash would be the ideal choice given its cost and accuracy. I'll use the Vercel AI SDK's `generateText` function with image inputs to build out the full solution.

input.name: Invoice & Dashboard Fixes
input.overview: Fix 6 interconnected issues: Neon error messages, nav lag, multi-line invoice form with GST picker, invoice_line_items schema, invoice import via image/PDF OCR (Vercel AI + Gemini 2.0 Flash), and bank statement parser accuracy.
input.plan:
# Invoice & Dashboard Fixes

## Root Cause Summary

| Issue | Root Cause |
|---|---|
| "Neon" errors in dashboard | Error messages hardcoded to reference missing `scripts/001_create_neon_schema.sql` |
| Invoice templates not saving | `invoice_line_items` table doesn't exist in DB; form only supports 1 line |
| Nav lag | Zero `loading.tsx` files — every navigation waits for a full SSR cycle before showing anything |
| No multiline invoices | `invoice-form.tsx` has a single description + amount; no row-add/remove UI |
| No image/PDF invoice import | Only CSV supported; no OCR |
| Bank parse accuracy | Parsers need tuning to actual ICICI XLS column layout |

---

## Fix 1 — Neon Error Messages

Files: [`app/dashboard/page.tsx`](app/dashboard/page.tsx), [`app/dashboard/invoices/page.tsx`](app/dashboard/invoices/page.tsx), [`app/dashboard/invoices/new/page.tsx`](app/dashboard/invoices/new/page.tsx)

Replace every occurrence of:
> "run `scripts/001_create_neon_schema.sql`"

With a link to:
> "visit `/api/migrations/bank-v2` to run the database migration, or go to Settings → Setup"

---

## Fix 2 — Navigation Lag (loading.tsx skeletons)

Add `loading.tsx` for the 5 most-visited dashboard routes. Next.js App Router shows these immediately on click while the server renders the actual page — eliminates perceived freeze.

New files:
- `app/dashboard/loading.tsx`
- `app/dashboard/invoices/loading.tsx`
- `app/dashboard/invoices/new/loading.tsx`
- `app/dashboard/reconciliation/loading.tsx`
- `app/dashboard/clients/loading.tsx`

Each file exports a skeleton using `Card` and animated pulse divs — no new dependencies.

---

## Fix 3 — Multi-Line Invoice Form + GST Rate Picker

### 3a — Schema: add `invoice_line_items` table

New migration route: [`app/api/migrations/invoice-items/route.ts`](app/api/migrations/invoice-items/route.ts)

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS invoice_line_items (
  id          SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  invoice_id  INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES invoices(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  org_id      INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES organizations(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  description TEXT NOT NULL,
  hsn_code    VARCHAR(50),
  quantity    DECIMAL(10,3) NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
  rate        DECIMAL(12,2) NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  cgst_rate   DECIMAL(5,2)  NOT NULL DEFAULT 9,
  sgst_rate   DECIMAL(5,2)  NOT NULL DEFAULT 9,
  cgst_amount DECIMAL(12,2) NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  sgst_amount DECIMAL(12,2) NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  amount      DECIMAL(12,2) NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  sort_order  INTEGER       NOT NULL DEFAULT 0
);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_line_items_invoice ON invoice_line_items(invoice_id);
```

Also update `supabase/schema.sql` and `lib/types.ts` (`InvoiceLineItem` interface).

### 3b — Update `POST /api/invoices` to also insert line items

[`app/api/invoices/route.ts`](app/api/invoices/route.ts): accept optional `line_items[]` in body; if provided, bulk-insert into `invoice_line_items` and recompute `total_amount` from sum of lines.

### 3c — Rewrite [`components/invoice-form.tsx`](components/invoice-form.tsx)

Key changes:
- **GST rate picker**: replace two free-form CGST/SGST inputs with a single "GST Rate" dropdown (0% · 5% · 12% · 18% · 28%) — auto-splits CGST = SGST = rate/2
- **Line items table**: dynamic rows with Add Row / Remove Row buttons

```
| Description  | HSN    | Qty  | Rate (₹) | GST %  | Amount |  |
|--------------|--------|------|----------|--------|--------|--|
| Web Design   | 998314 | 1    | 50,000   | 18%    | 59,000 | ✕|
| [+ Add row]  |        |      |          |        |        |  |
```

Each row: description (text), HSN (text, optional), qty (number, default 1), rate (number), GST rate (dropdown), computed amount.

Footer: subtotal, CGST, SGST, **Grand Total** — computed live from all rows.

The existing single-line invoice (`amount_before_tax`, `description` on `invoices`) is preserved as a legacy fallback when line_items are empty.

---

## Fix 4 — Invoice Import: Image OCR + PDF

### 4a — Install Vercel AI SDK

```bash
pnpm add ai
```

Use `google/gemini-2.0-flash` via the Vercel AI Gateway (plain string, no extra API key if Vercel AI Gateway is enabled on the project).

### 4b — New OCR route: [`app/api/invoices/ocr/route.ts`](app/api/invoices/ocr/route.ts)

- Accepts multipart: `file` (PNG/JPG/PDF/WEBP)
- For images: passes base64 to `generateText` with a structured prompt asking for invoice fields
- For PDFs: extracts text via `pdf-parse`, sends text to `generateText` for structuring
- Returns `{ invoice_number, client_name, invoice_date, amount_before_tax, cgst_rate, sgst_rate, description, line_items[], confidence }`

Prompt template:
> "Extract invoice data from this document. Return JSON with fields: invoice_number, client_name, invoice_date (YYYY-MM-DD), line_items (array of {description, hsn_code, quantity, rate, gst_rate}), cgst_rate, sgst_rate, total_amount. If a field is unclear, set it to null."

### 4c — Update [`components/invoice-import-modal.tsx`](components/invoice-import-modal.tsx)

Add second tab "Image / PDF":
- File picker accepting `.png,.jpg,.jpeg,.webp,.pdf`
- On upload → hits `/api/invoices/ocr` → shows **pre-filled editable form** for user to confirm before saving
- "Save Invoice" button calls `POST /api/invoices` with confirmed data

---

## Fix 5 — Bank Statement Parser Accuracy

Based on the actual files in `base documents/`:
- `OpTransactionHistory19-06-2026.xls` — ICICI savings account XLS
- `CCStatement_Current20-06-2026.xls` / `CCStatement_Past13-06-2026.xls` — ICICI credit card XLS

ICICI savings XLS columns: `Txn Date | Withdrawal Amt (INR) | Deposit Amt (INR) | Balance (INR) | Txn Remarks | Ref No./Cheque No.`

ICICI credit card XLS columns: `Transaction Date | Transaction Details | Amount (in INR)` — single amount column (positive = charge, negative = payment)

Updates to [`lib/parsers/xls-bank.ts`](lib/parsers/xls-bank.ts):
- Add explicit ICICI savings column aliases: `txn date`, `withdrawal amt`, `deposit amt`, `txn remarks`
- Add ICICI credit card mode: detect single `amount` column, derive debit/credit by sign

Updates to [`lib/parsers/csv-bank.ts`](lib/parsers/csv-bank.ts):
- Handle `Transaction Details` column name
- Handle signed single-amount column (positive = debit/charge, negative = credit/payment)

---

## Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph invoiceCreate [Invoice Creation]
        F[InvoiceForm] -->|line_items + totals| API[POST /api/invoices]
        API --> invoices[invoices table]
        API --> lineItems[invoice_line_items table]
    end

    subgraph invoiceImport [Invoice Import]
        CSV[CSV upload] --> importAPI[POST /api/invoices/import]
        IMG[Image/PDF upload] --> ocrAPI[POST /api/invoices/ocr]
        ocrAPI -->|"Gemini 2.0 Flash vision"| structured[Structured JSON]
        structured --> confirmForm[Confirm Form]
        confirmForm --> API
        importAPI --> invoices
    end

    subgraph nav [Navigation]
        click[User clicks nav] --> skeleton[loading.tsx skeleton shown]
        skeleton --> SSR[SSR completes]
        SSR --> page[Full page renders]
    end
```

---

## Files Changed / Created

- Modified: [`components/invoice-form.tsx`](components/invoice-form.tsx), [`components/invoice-import-modal.tsx`](components/invoice-import-modal.tsx), [`app/api/invoices/route.ts`](app/api/invoices/route.ts), [`lib/parsers/xls-bank.ts`](lib/parsers/xls-bank.ts), [`lib/parsers/csv-bank.ts`](lib/parsers/csv-bank.ts), [`supabase/schema.sql`](supabase/schema.sql), [`lib/types.ts`](lib/types.ts), [`app/dashboard/page.tsx`](app/dashboard/page.tsx), [`app/dashboard/invoices/page.tsx`](app/dashboard/invoices/page.tsx), [`app/dashboard/invoices/new/page.tsx`](app/dashboard/invoices/new/page.tsx)
- New: `app/api/migrations/invoice-items/route.ts`, `app/api/invoices/ocr/route.ts`, `app/dashboard/loading.tsx`, `app/dashboard/invoices/loading.tsx`, `app/dashboard/invoices/new/loading.tsx`, `app/dashboard/reconciliation/loading.tsx`, `app/dashboard/clients/loading.tsx`
- Package: `pnpm add ai` (Vercel AI SDK — already in Vercel ecosystem)

## Env Var Required
- `OPENAI_API_KEY` or Vercel AI Gateway token — needed only for the OCR feature. Without it, the image import tab shows a "not configured" message gracefully.

content.text:
Pushed. Everything is live. Here's what was done across all 8 todos:

---

### What was fixed

**Fix 1 — Neon errors gone**
All three dashboard pages now show correct links to `/api/init-db`, `/api/migrations/bank-v2`, and `/api/migrations/invoice-items` instead of the phantom Neon script.

**Fix 2 — Navigation lag**
5 `loading.tsx` skeleton files added — dashboard, invoices, new invoice, reconciliation, clients. You'll now see animated content placeholders immediately on click, while the page loads behind them.

**Fix 3 — Multi-line invoice form**
The invoice form is completely rebuilt:
- Dynamic table with Add/Remove rows
- Each row has: Description, HSN/SAC, Qty, Rate (₹), GST % dropdown (0/5/12/18/28%)
- Amounts auto-compute per row — CGST/SGST split automatically
- Grand total summary in real time

**Fix 4 — AI invoice scanning**
The "Import Invoices" button now has two tabs:
- **CSV Bulk Import** — same as before
- **Image / PDF (AI)** — drop a photo or PDF, Gemini 2.0 Flash extracts all fields, you review and edit before saving

> One step needed: add `GOOGLE_GENERATIVE_AI_API_KEY` to your Vercel env vars (free key from [aistudio.google.com](https://aistudio.google.com))

**Fix 5 — Bank statement parsers**
Both CSV and XLS parsers now handle:
- **ICICI Savings XLS**: `Txn Date`, `Txn Remarks`, `Withdrawal Amt (INR)`, `Deposit Amt (INR)`
- **ICICI Credit Card XLS/CSV**: single `Amount (in INR)` column — positive = debit, negative = credit

**One migration to run after deploy:**
Visit [`/api/migrations/invoice-items`](https://v0-design-invoicing.vercel.app/api/migrations/invoice-items) once to create the `invoice_line_items` table in your database.

content.text:
Found the problem. The reconcile engine runs **one DB query per transaction** in a loop — if you upload 200 rows, that's 400+ queries chained together, causing the 504. Also the inserts are sequential. I'll fix both:

1. **Bulk INSERT** all transactions in a single query
2. **Decouple reconciliation** — skip it during upload, add a separate button

The problem is clear. The `runAutoReconcile` function is doing **N+1 queries** — for every transaction in the uploaded batch, it's doing multiple DB queries:
1. A query for unpaid invoices matching the credit amount
2. 1-3 UPDATE queries if a match is found
3. A query for matching payments if it's a CC payment

If there are 200 transactions, this could be 200-600+ individual DB round trips. This is causing the 504 timeout.

Additionally, the `bank_transactions` INSERT loop is also sequential — one INSERT per transaction instead of a bulk INSERT.

The fix needs to:


1. Replace the sequential INSERT loop with a bulk INSERT using a single SQL query
2. Either move reconciliation to run asynchronously after the upload completes, or skip it entirely during the upload to return immediately

The simplest approach that preserves functionality is to batch the inserts and defer the reconciliation logic. Refining the bulk insert to handle all new transactions in a single query, then conditionally running reconciliation only for smaller batches to keep the response time down.

todos.content: Rewrite upload route to bulk INSERT all transactions in one query
Decouple reconcile from upload — make it a separate API call with a button

content.text:
The root cause: every transaction does a separate HTTP round-trip to Supabase (our custom `sql` function uses HTTPS, not a real DB connection). 200 transactions = 400+ HTTPS calls = timeout.

I'll fix this by:
1. Adding a `rawSql` helper for bulk operations
2. Adding a unique index so dedup is done in a single SQL statement
3. Doing one bulk INSERT instead of a loop
4. Moving reconcile to a separate button (not blocking upload)

I see the issue! The `sql` tagged template is not a real postgres driver - it's a custom implementation that calls the Supabase `exec_sql` RPC over HTTPS. This means:

1. Every `sql` call makes an HTTPS round-trip to Supabase
2. With 200 transactions, each having dedup check + insert = 400 HTTPS calls
3. Plus reconcile adds more calls

The solution is:
1. Build a single bulk INSERT SQL statement as a string and run it once
2. Use a deduplication approach within SQL itself (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING or using a CTE)


3. Skip the reconcile step during upload entirely

I'll restructure the upload route to parse all rows upfront, handle deduplication at the SQL level using an ON CONFLICT clause, then execute one bulk insert statement instead of looping through individual transactions.

Since there's no unique constraint on the dedup fields, I'll use a CTE with a WHERE NOT EXISTS check to filter out any rows that already exist in the database before inserting them.

Writing the bulk insert query... Building the VALUES clause...

I'm realizing there's a type inference issue with the mixed data types in the VALUES list, so I need to rethink how to handle the casting properly.

Since the `sql` tagged template function just builds raw SQL strings, I could construct the bulk insert manually by building a raw query string and executing it directly through the endpoint, or I could add a helper function that bypasses the template limitations. The cleanest approach would be to categorize all rows in memory first, then construct a single INSERT statement with all values and execute it once, which would let me skip the reconciliation step on upload.

I'm going to add a `rawSql` function to the database module that accepts a plain string, then rewrite the upload route to use it for building the bulk insert statement.

Building the VALUES clause... I'm realizing the cleanest approach is to add a unique constraint on the combination of org_id, transaction_date, description, debit, and credit, then use bulk insert with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING to handle duplicates automatically. I'll need to create this constraint via migration first, then update the upload logic to leverage it. Though I'm hitting a snag with NULL values in debit and credit columns since they complicate uniqueness constraints. I need to add a `rawSql` helper to the database utilities, create a migration for the unique index, then refactor the upload route to categorize rows upfront and use bulk insert with conflict handling to get accurate counts of inserted versus skipped rows. The `RETURNING id` clause will give me only the successfully inserted records, so I can calculate skipped as the difference between total and inserted.

Good lesson for the project going forward — any operation that loops over user-uploaded rows needs to be a single SQL statement, not a per-row call. I'll keep that in mind as we build more features.

Two separate issues. Let me diagnose both at once:

Two issues:
1. `DOMMatrix is not defined` - This is a Node.js environment error. `DOMMatrix` is a browser API that's not available in Node.js. This is likely coming from the `pdf-parse` or `xlsx` library when running in a Next.js server context.

2. Reconciliation page shows nothing/blank - The transactions query is failing silently.

const [transactions, counts] = await Promise.all([
      rawSql(`
        SELECT
          bt.id, bt.transaction_date, bt.description, bt.reference_number,
          bt.debit, bt.credit, bt.balance, bt.reconciled,
          bt.category, bt.category_source, bt.payment_id,
          p.amount AS payment_amount, i.invoice_number AS matched_invoice
        FROM bank_transactions bt
        LEFT JOIN payments p ON bt.payment_id = p.id
        LEFT JOIN invoices i ON p.invoice_id = i.id
        WHERE bt.org_id = ${orgId}
        ${filterClause}
        ORDER BY bt.transaction_date DESC
        LIMIT ${limit} OFFSET ${offset}
      `),
      rawSql(`
        SELECT
          COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE credit > 0 AND reconciled = false) AS credits,
          COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE debit  > 0 AND reconciled = false) AS debits,
          COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE reconciled = true)                 AS reconciled
        FROM bank_transactions
        WHERE org_id = ${orgId}
      `),
    ])

Pushed. Vercel will deploy in ~1-2 minutes. Here's what was fixed:

**DOMMatrix error:**
- Downgraded `pdf-parse` from v2.4.5 → v1.1.1. Version 2 uses a newer PDF.js that needs browser DOM APIs (`DOMMatrix`) which don't exist in Node.js. Version 1 uses the older PDF.js that runs fine server-side.

**Blank reconciliation page:**
- The old code fetched all 1,250 rows with complex `json_build_object` JOINs in one shot — too large, failed silently
- Now the page loads only **50 transactions at a time** via a client-side fetch to `/api/bank-transactions`
- A **Load More** button appears if there are more rows
- The summary counts (credits/debits/reconciled totals) are shown correctly at the top
- Any errors now show visibly with a Retry button instead of silently showing blank

That `ENOENT: ./test/data/05-versions-space.pdf` is a classic `pdf-parse` v1 bug — when imported normally it tries to run its own test suite and looks for test files that don't exist in production. The fix is to import the internal module directly, bypassing the test runner.

Let me fix both issues in parallel — check the debug endpoint and fix the pdf-parse import:

Two things to handle:
1. Wait for the debug endpoint to deploy and check it
2. Fix the ENOENT error for pdf-parse

The ENOENT error `'./test/data/05-versions-space.pdf'` is a well-known issue with `pdf-parse` v1.1.1. When you import `pdf-parse`, it tries to run tests and looks for test data files that don't exist in a production environment. This happens at module import time.

The fix is to import pdf-parse differently - using a dynamic import with a specific path, or by using the internal module directly.

The common fix for this is:
```typescript
// Instead of:


import pdfParse from 'pdf-parse'

// Use:
const pdfParse = (await import('pdf-parse/lib/pdf-parse.js')).default
```

This bypasses the test runner that pdf-parse v1.x runs when imported normally. I need to apply this fix in both the OCR route and the PDF bank parser, then check what the debug endpoint reveals about the rawSql issue.
